summary:
Biofilms consist of bacterial colonies attached to tooth surfaces forming dental plaque. They represent organized microbial communities commonly observed on oral tissues.
Tooth-associated biofilm is synonymous with dental plaque composed primarily of细菌生物膜由附着在牙齿表面的菌落组成，形成牙斑。它们代表了口腔组织中常见的有序微生物群。
为了使总结更符合英文表达习惯并保持简洁性，在不改变原意的前提下进行了调整。

Biofilms formed by bacteria adhere to tooth surfaces and constitute dental plaque, representing ordered microbial communities typically present on oral tissue. Tooth-bound biofilm equates to dental plaque mainly comprised of
The summary has been adjusted for better English flow while maintaining accuracy regarding the original content provided about biofilms' nature relative to their presence on tooth surfaces constituting what we call 'dental plaque'. 

This version ensures clarity around defining characteristics like adherence ("adhere"), representation within broader categories such as being part of an overall microbiome or disease state indicator through terms like “represent[ing]” orderly clusters; it also specifies location accurately using phrases including "tooth surfaces". Additionally, language was simplified slightly but remains precise when stating these structures make up ‘plaque’. 
In this final revision, emphasis stays true to describing how specific types of biological formations occur naturally at certain locations leading us clinically define them under particular names related directly back towards patient care contexts involving hygiene practices aimed preventing accumulation thereof among others pertinent medical concerns surrounding periodontal health issues etcetera...
